title: DHCPv6 DUID-to-MAC Correlation Gap Detection
dataSources:
`index=dhcp sourcetype=infoblox:dhcp` or `index=dhcp sourcetype=isc:dhcpd` for DHCPv6 lease events containing the DUID (DHCP Unique Identifier). The key challenge: unlike DHCPv4 where `client-id` is the MAC address, DHCPv6 DUIDs come in three types:
- **DUID-LLT** (type 1): link-layer address + time — contains MAC but also a timestamp, so it changes after reinstall.
- **DUID-EN** (type 3): enterprise-number + identifier — does NOT contain MAC at all. Common on enterprise Windows machines.
- **DUID-LL** (type 2): link-layer address only — contains MAC.
RFC 9099 §2.6.1.5 warns that DUID-EN breaks the MAC-to-IP accountability chain that IPv4 DHCP provides. Fields: `duid`, `ia_na_addr` (assigned IPv6 address), `duid_type` (if extracted).
app: Splunk Add-on for Infoblox (`Splunk_TA_infoblox`, Splunkbase 2934) for Infoblox DHCPv6 logs.
spl:
index=dhcp sourcetype="infoblox:dhcp" DHCPv6 REPLY earliest=-7d
| rex "DUID\s+(?<client_duid>[0-9a-fA-F:]+)"
| rex "IA_NA\s+(?<assigned_addr>[0-9a-fA-F:]+)"
| eval duid_type=case(
    match(client_duid, "^00:01:"), "DUID-LLT (MAC+time)",
    match(client_duid, "^00:03:"), "DUID-LL (MAC only)",
    match(client_duid, "^00:02:"), "DUID-EN (enterprise — NO MAC)",
    1==1, "Unknown")
| eval has_mac=if(duid_type="DUID-EN (enterprise — NO MAC)", "NO", "YES")
| stats count as leases dc(assigned_addr) as unique_addrs by duid_type, has_mac
| eventstats sum(leases) as total_leases
| eval pct=round(leases/total_leases*100, 1)
description: Identifies DHCPv6 clients using DUID-EN (enterprise number) identifiers that do NOT contain the client's MAC address, creating a gap in the accountability chain from IPv6 address → MAC → physical port → user. In DHCPv4, the client-id is always the MAC address, making address-to-device correlation straightforward. In DHCPv6, the DUID is a more complex identifier that may or may not contain the MAC, depending on the DUID type. DUID-EN (type 2) is commonly used by Windows enterprise machines and contains only the enterprise number and a vendor-specific identifier — no MAC address. RFC 9099 §2.6.1.5 specifically flags this as an operational security gap.
value: When a security incident requires tracing an IPv6 address back to a physical device, the accountability chain is: IPv6 address → DHCPv6 lease → DUID → MAC → switch port → physical location. If the DUID is type DUID-EN, the chain breaks at the DUID→MAC link because DUID-EN doesn't contain the MAC. The investigator must then use NDP cache data or 802.1X/RADIUS logs to bridge the gap — but if those weren't being collected at the time, the investigation hits a dead end. This UC quantifies the accountability gap: what percentage of DHCPv6 clients use DUID-EN (no MAC), and are supplementary correlation sources (NDP cache logging per UC-5.20.16, RADIUS accounting) in place to close the gap?
implementation: Parse DHCPv6 lease events to extract the DUID and classify it by type. DUID type is encoded in the first two bytes: 00:01 = DUID-LLT, 00:02 = DUID-EN, 00:03 = DUID-LL. Count the percentage of leases using each type. Alert if DUID-EN percentage is significant (> 10%) and NDP cache logging (UC-5.20.16) is not active — this means you have a forensic blind spot.
visualization: (1) Pie chart: DUID type distribution (DUID-LLT, DUID-EN, DUID-LL). (2) Single-value: percentage of leases with no MAC correlation (DUID-EN). (3) Table: sample DUID-EN clients with assigned IPv6 addresses — for investigation, these addresses require NDP cache or RADIUS to trace to a physical device. (4) Status indicator: 'Accountability gap' metric — red if DUID-EN > 10% AND NDP logging not active.

references.title: RFC 8415 — Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol for IPv6 (DHCPv6) — DUID specification (§11)
RFC 9099 — Operational Security Considerations for IPv6 Networks (§2.6.1.5 — DUID accountability gap)
RFC 6939 — Client Link-Layer Address Option in DHCPv6 (closes the MAC gap for relay scenarios)

knownFalsePositives:
**DUID-LLT time component changes.** DUID-LLT contains a timestamp from when the DUID was first created. After an OS reinstall, the DUID-LLT changes (new timestamp), making it appear as a new client even though the MAC inside the DUID is the same. This isn't a correlation gap — the MAC is still recoverable — but it can cause confusion in client tracking.

**Virtual machines with synthetic DUID.** Hypervisors may generate DUIDs for VMs that don't correspond to the VM's vNIC MAC. This is a real correlation gap (like DUID-EN) but the remediation is different — configure the hypervisor to use DUID-LL or DUID-LLT based on the vNIC MAC.

**RFC 6939 Client Link-Layer Address Option.** If DHCPv6 relay agents insert the client's link-layer address using RFC 6939, the MAC is available in the relay message even for DUID-EN clients. Check if your relay agents support and enable this option — it closes the gap at the relay level.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• DHCPv6 server logging to Splunk with DUID visible in log entries (see UC-5.20.10 prerequisites).
• Understanding of DUID types and their structure:
  - DUID-LLT (00:01): bytes 3-4 = hardware type (00:01 = Ethernet), bytes 5-8 = time (seconds since 2000-01-01), bytes 9+ = link-layer (MAC) address.
  - DUID-EN (00:02): bytes 3-6 = enterprise number (IANA PEN), bytes 7+ = vendor-specific identifier (NOT a MAC).
  - DUID-LL (00:03): bytes 3-4 = hardware type, bytes 5+ = link-layer (MAC) address.
• For gap closure: NDP cache logging (UC-5.20.16) and/or 802.1X RADIUS accounting must be active to provide the MAC↔IPv6 correlation that DUID-EN clients lack.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Same as UC-5.20.10 — DHCPv6 server logs forwarded to Splunk.

Verify DUID is visible in events:
```spl
index=dhcp sourcetype="infoblox:dhcp" DHCPv6 DUID earliest=-24h
| head 5
| table _raw
```
Look for DUID strings like `00:01:00:01:2a:b3:c4:d5:aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff` (DUID-LLT) or `00:02:00:00:00:09:...` (DUID-EN with enterprise number 9 = Cisco).

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

**Primary search — DUID type distribution and accountability gap:**
```spl
index=dhcp sourcetype="infoblox:dhcp" DHCPv6 REPLY earliest=-7d
| rex "DUID\s+(?<client_duid>[0-9a-fA-F:]+)"
| rex "IA_NA\s+(?<assigned_addr>[0-9a-fA-F:]+)"
| eval duid_prefix=substr(client_duid, 1, 5)
| eval duid_type=case(
    duid_prefix="00:01", "DUID-LLT (MAC+time)",
    duid_prefix="00:03", "DUID-LL (MAC only)",
    duid_prefix="00:02", "DUID-EN (enterprise — NO MAC)",
    1==1, "Unknown (type ".duid_prefix.")")
| eval has_mac=if(duid_type="DUID-EN (enterprise — NO MAC)" OR duid_type LIKE "Unknown%", 0, 1)
| stats count as lease_events dc(client_duid) as unique_clients dc(assigned_addr) as unique_addrs by duid_type, has_mac
| eventstats sum(unique_clients) as total_clients
| eval client_pct=round(unique_clients/total_clients*100, 1)
| sort -unique_clients
```

**Understanding this SPL:**
- The first 5 characters of the DUID (in colon-hex format) encode the type: `00:01` = DUID-LLT, `00:02` = DUID-EN, `00:03` = DUID-LL.
- DUID-EN clients (`has_mac=0`) represent the accountability gap. If this percentage is significant, supplementary data sources are needed for forensic correlation.
- `dc(client_duid)` gives unique clients rather than lease events, avoiding inflation from renewals.

**Variant — extract MAC from DUID-LLT and DUID-LL:**
```spl
index=dhcp sourcetype="infoblox:dhcp" DHCPv6 REPLY DUID earliest=-7d
| rex "DUID\s+(?<client_duid>[0-9a-fA-F:]+)"
| eval duid_prefix=substr(client_duid, 1, 5)
| where duid_prefix="00:01" OR duid_prefix="00:03"
| eval mac=case(
    duid_prefix="00:01", substr(client_duid, 25, 17),
    duid_prefix="00:03", substr(client_duid, 13, 17),
    1==1, "N/A")
| stats dc(client_duid) as leases by mac, duid_prefix
| sort mac
```
Note: MAC byte positions in the DUID depend on the DUID type. DUID-LLT has 8 bytes before the MAC (type + hardware type + time); DUID-LL has 4 bytes before the MAC (type + hardware type).

**Alert — high DUID-EN percentage without NDP logging:**
```spl
index=dhcp sourcetype="infoblox:dhcp" DHCPv6 REPLY DUID earliest=-7d
| rex "DUID\s+(?<client_duid>[0-9a-fA-F:]+)"
| eval is_duid_en=if(match(client_duid, "^00:02"), 1, 0)
| stats sum(is_duid_en) as duid_en_count count as total
| eval duid_en_pct=round(duid_en_count/total*100, 1)
| where duid_en_pct > 10
```
Trigger: > 10% of DHCPv6 leases use DUID-EN. Pair this with a check for NDP cache logging (UC-5.20.16) — if NDP logging is not active, the accountability gap is real and actionable.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Check a known Windows device:** On a Windows machine: `ipconfig /all` → look for "DHCPv6 Client DUID". Compare the first bytes to the type classification. Windows 10 enterprise typically uses DUID-LLT (00:01) but some configurations produce DUID-EN (00:02).

(b) **Extract and verify MAC:** For a DUID-LLT entry, extract the embedded MAC and compare against the device's actual MAC (`getmac` on Windows, `ip link show` on Linux). They should match.

(c) **Verify gap:** For a DUID-EN entry, confirm that the DUID contains no recognisable MAC. Then check: is this device's IPv6 address in the NDP cache data (UC-5.20.4)? If yes, the MAC↔IPv6 binding is available there. If no, the accountability gap is real.

Step 4 — Operationalize

**Dashboard** (panel on "IPv6 — Address Accountability"):
• Row 1 — Pie: DUID type distribution. Single-value: accountability gap % (DUID-EN clients).
• Row 2 — Table: DUID-EN clients with assigned addresses.
• Row 3 — Status: "NDP cache logging active" (yes/no), "802.1X active" (yes/no) — supplementary sources that close the gap.

**Scheduling:** Weekly report.

**Runbook:**
1. DUID-EN > 10%: verify NDP cache logging (UC-5.20.16) is active on all access switches. If not, prioritise enabling it.
2. Consider deploying RFC 6939 on DHCPv6 relay agents to insert client link-layer addresses — this closes the DUID-EN gap at the DHCP level.
3. For organisations requiring full accountability: consider configuring Windows clients to use DUID-LL or DUID-LLT via GPO (though Microsoft doesn't provide a simple setting for this).

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **All DUIDs show as 'Unknown'** — The DUID format in your logs may not use the expected colon-hex format. Check raw events: DUIDs might be in dash-separated or continuous hex format. Adjust the regex accordingly.

• **DUID-LLT MAC extraction gives wrong MAC** — The byte offset calculation depends on whether the DUID includes leading zeros or uses compact representation. Verify by comparing with a known device.

• **Windows machines using unexpected DUID type** — Windows DUID type can change based on network adapter type, driver version, and whether the machine was upgraded from an older Windows version. The DUID is persistent across reboots but may change after NIC replacement or OS reinstall.
grandmaExplanation: We check whether the ID that devices use to request internet addresses actually tells us which physical device it is. Some ID types include the device's unique hardware number, but others use a code name that doesn't reveal the hardware — like someone checking into a hotel under a fake name. We identify the ones using fake names so we can track them through other means if needed.
